case_name: Professional Conduct Committee appointed by the Podiatrist Board of New Zealand v Koh [2020] NZHPDT 1100 (20 July 2020)

summary.brief: Professional Conduct Committee appointed by the Podiatrist Board of New Zealand v Koh [2020] NZHPDT 1100 (20 July 2020). Medical malpractice case involving other.

evidence_index.text: On 31 July 2018 Brian Libberto Koh pleaded guilty to three representative charges of dishonestly using a document, contrary to s 228(b) of the Crimes Act 1961
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: 23 instances of fabricating accident details; seven instances of forging a client’s signature on ACC lodgement forms; 13 instances of forging a client’s signature on his workplace consent forms
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: 39 instances of charging ACC for one hour treatments when he was aware the treatment given was between 10 and 15 minutes
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: charging ACC for non-existent follow-up treatments for sports event participants and “walk-ins”.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The above conduct amounts to professional misconduct in that it: (a) amounts to malpractice or negligence in relation to his scope of practice
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: has brought or is likely to bring discredit to the profession, pursuant to section 100(1)(b) of the Act.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

focused_exam.system: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

decision_points.prompt: What should have been documented for ACC claims?

options.description: Accurate documentation of treatment duration and patient consent
options.clinical_reasoning: Ensures transparency and compliance with ACC requirements

options.description: Fabricate treatment details and forge signatures
options.clinical_reasoning: Misleading and fraudulent, leading to misconduct charges

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant fabricated treatment details and forged signatures

expected_action_court.description: Accurate documentation should have been maintained
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Legal and ethical standards for professional conduct

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Fabrication and forgery are unethical and illegal

key_findings: Breach of duty in fabricating treatment details

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Accurate documentation would have prevented misconduct charges

factual_timeline.event: Brian Libberto Koh pleaded guilty to charges

allegations.allegation: Fabrication of treatment details and forgery

court_findings.finding: Professional misconduct due to fabrication and forgery
